title: I Stopped Typing to OpenClaw — Typeless voice dictation
summary: Typeless is an AI voice dictation tool that enables users to compose and control applications via speech input at significantly higher speeds than typing. The tool supports multilingual content creation, AI-assisted transcription, and integration with other applications through voice-controlled actions.
writeup: A walkthrough of Typeless, a voice dictation tool the presenter uses 4-5 hours daily, covering its three core modes: dictate (voice-to-text with automatic filler removal and formatting), ask (an embedded AI agent that can search, rewrite, and transform highlighted text without copy-pasting), and translate (real-time multilingual output including Cantonese-English code-switching). The central argument is that voice at 198 words per minute removes the friction of keyboard input and copy-paste loops, and that embedding AI actions directly into the voice layer turns dictation into genuine voice control. The video walks through desktop setup with Stream Deck hotkeys, personalization settings, a mobile keyboard demo, and several real workflows including brainstorming, social post generation, and YouTube description drafting.
key_points: Typeless operates in three distinct modes triggered by separate hotkeys: dictate (transcribes and formats speech, stripping filler words), ask (sends a voice prompt to an AI agent that acts on the current context), and translate (outputs multilingual text including mixed Cantonese-English speech).

The presenter dictates at 198 words per minute versus a 40-wpm typing baseline, and the compounded cost of copy-paste context switches across 4-5 hours of daily AI use is the core motivation for switching to voice-first workflows.

The ask function acts as an embedded AI agent on highlighted text: demonstrated uses include rewriting a Threads post into 3-5 hook variations, stripping hashtags while adding emojis, generating a YouTube description from a transcript, and searching for real-time results, all without opening a separate chat interface.

Typeless adapts to writing style automatically over time through a personalization layer, and a custom dictionary lets users add proper nouns and domain terms (such as OpenAI or a unique personal name) to prevent mis-transcription.
The presenter integrates Typeless with a Stream Deck so a single physical button press activates dictate, ask, or translate, eliminating the need to reach for a keyboard hotkey during a workflow.
A live demo shows Typeless correctly handling Cantonese-English code-switching for a Threads post, which the presenter identifies as a distinguishing capability absent from competing voice tools.
